scenario: A patient underlying rheumatoid arthritis with pale conjunctiva. MCV 78, ferritin 150 ng/ml (normal 180-270 ng/ml), serum iron binding capacity 100 mcg/dL (normal 240-450 mcg/dL). What is the most likely cause?
ai_notes: Patient with rheumatoid arthritis (chronic disease) presents with microcytic anemia (MCV 78). Low ferritin and low TIBC are characteristic of anemia of chronic inflammation/disease (also called anemia of chronic disease). This is the most common cause of anemia in chronic inflammatory conditions. The low ferritin is due to hepcidin-induced iron sequestration, not true iron deficiency.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 3-year-old boy presents with dark urine 1 day. He has had fever 2 days ago. No medication. He has history of neonatal jaundice. BT 38°C, P 120. PE: mild jaundice, icteric sclera, no hepatosplenomegaly. PBS: Hct 28%, normochromic, normocytic anemia with Hb leakage from RBC. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Classic presentation of G6PD deficiency: history of neonatal jaundice, acute hemolytic episode triggered by fever, dark urine (hemoglobinuria), normocytic normochromic anemia with hemoglobin leakage from RBCs ('bite cells' or 'blister cells' on PBS). Fever is a common trigger for hemolysis in G6PD deficiency.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 25-year-old female complains of being easily fatigued for 2 months. PE: moderately pale, no jaundice, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly. CBC: Hb 9 g/dL, WBC 5,500 (N 60, L 35, M 5), platelet 650,000, hypochromic microcytic red cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Classic presentation of iron deficiency anemia: chronic fatigue, hypochromic microcytic RBCs, elevated platelet count (reactive thrombocytosis is common in IDA), no jaundice (distinguishes from hemolytic anemias), normal WBC. The 2-month duration suggests chronic blood loss or nutritional deficiency.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 19-year-old boy came to hospital with mild fever and fatigue 3 days. PE: mild pale conjunctiva, mild icteric sclera, other WNL. CBC: Hb 8 g/dL, WBC 4200 (N 40%, L 60%). PBS: hemoglobin contracted cell, ghost cell, polychromasia 2+. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: G6PD deficiency: fever as trigger, hemoglobin contracted cells (Heinz bodies), ghost cells (from oxidative damage), polychromasia indicating reticulocytosis (response to hemolysis). The clinical picture of acute hemolytic episode with fever in a young male is typical for G6PD deficiency.

scenario: A 10-year-old girl comes with fatigue for 1 week. PE: BT 38°C, moderate pallor, spleen 1 cm below left costal margin. CBC: Hct 21%, WBC 4000 (N 60, L 40), platelet 250,000. PBS: normochromic normocytic RBC, polychromasia 2+, spherocyte 3+. Direct antiglobulin test positive. Reticulocyte count 10%. What is the most appropriate management?
ai_notes: This is AIHA (Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia): DAT positive, spherocytes, elevated reticulocyte count, normochromic normocytic anemia. First-line management is corticosteroids (Prednisolone). Splenectomy is considered for steroid-dependent cases. IVIG is used in emergency situations or steroid-resistant cases.

scenario: A 65-year-old man has progressive fatigue for 3 months. PE: marked pale, mild jaundice, no hepatosplenomegaly, no superficial lymphadenopathy. Hb 14 g/dL, MCV 120, platelet 65,000. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Megaloblastic anemia: macrocytic anemia (MCV 120), pancytopenia with low platelets, mild jaundice (due to ineffective erythropoiesis), no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Caused by B12 or folate deficiency. The progressive fatigue over 3 months with macrocytosis and thrombocytopenia is classic for megaloblastic anemia.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A child whose father has Hb H disease and mother is α-thalassemia 1 trait. What is the risk the child will have a disease?
ai_notes: Hb H disease = deletion of 3 alpha-globin genes (--/-α). α-thal 1 trait = deletion of 2 alpha-globin genes (-α/-α). Father (Hb H: --/-α) can pass either -- or -α. Mother (α-thal 1: -α/-α) can only pass -α. Possible offspring: (--/-α) = Hb H disease 50% or (-α/-α) = α-thal 1 trait 50%. So 50% will have a disease phenotype.

scenario: A 30-year-old with fever for 3 weeks. Physical exam: spleen just palpable, cervical lymphadenopathy. Hb 7.5 g/dL, MCV 102, WBC 1.2 g/dL (N 7%, L 72%), platelet 30 × 10⁹. What is the cause?
ai_notes: Acute leukemia: pancytopenia (anemia, low WBC, low platelets), fever, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and macrocytosis. The combination of 3-week fever with lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and severe pancytopenia with macrocytosis is most consistent with acute leukemia (likely ALL given the lymph node involvement).

scenario: A 20-year-old boy, previously healthy, has hemarthrosis left knee after playing football. He never had this symptom before. Hct normal, WBC normal, platelet 150,000, PTT 50 sec, INR 1.1. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Hemophilia (A or B): spontaneous hemarthrosis after minor trauma in previously healthy young male is classic presentation. Prolonged PTT (50 sec) with normal INR and normal platelet count points to Factor VIII or IX deficiency. Hemophilia is the most common cause of spontaneous hemarthrosis in young males.

scenario: ผู้ป่วยหญิงมาด้วยอาการ hypermenorrhea ตรวจร่างกายพบ petechiae ตามตัว Platelet 10,000 Lab อื่นๆ ปกติ ถามว่า management ที่เหมาะสมที่สุด?
ai_notes: This is ITP (Immune Thrombocytopenia): severe thrombocytopenia (10,000) with petechiae and hypermenorrhea (bleeding manifestation). First-line treatment for newly diagnosed ITP is corticosteroids (Prednisolone). IVIG is second-line or used in emergency situations. Splenectomy is considered for chronic ITP.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 6-year-old boy bleeding after tooth extraction yesterday. PE: blood oozing from tooth-extracted wound and ecchymosis at buttock and lower extremities. CBC: Hct 30%, WBC 8500, platelet 250,000 with pale stain, PTT 30 sec, PT 11.5 sec. What is the best management?
ai_notes: This is Hemophilia A or B: prolonged PTT (30 sec is prolonged for a child), normal PT, normal platelet count. Pale staining platelets suggests they are normal. Bleeding after dental procedure is typical. Best immediate management is cryoprecipitate (contains Fibrinogen and Factor VIII). FFP is also acceptable but cryoprecipitate is more concentrated for factors VIII and fibrinogen.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A patient with hypertension and hyperthyroidism on medication regularly, presents with fever, sore throat, and low WBC. What is the cause of this symptom?
ai_notes: Antithyroid drugs (PTU, methimazole) are well-known to cause agranulocytosis or severe leukopenia. The fever and sore throat in this context suggest infection secondary to drug-induced leukopenia. This is a serious side effect of antithyroid medications requiring immediate cessation and monitoring.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 45-year-old female presents with bruise for 3 months. Physical examination reveals ecchymosis at forearm and leg. Lab: Hb 12 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, RDW 12, WBC 6,500, platelet 24,000. Blood smear normal except low platelet. HIV and Anti-HCV negative. What is the management?
ai_notes: This is chronic ITP: 3-month history of thrombocytopenia (24,000), ecchymosis, normal RBC and WBC, negative for secondary causes (HIV, HCV). First-line management for chronic ITP is corticosteroids (Prednisolone). Splenectomy is considered after steroid response assessment, usually in second line.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 50-year-old woman with alcoholic cirrhosis. BP 100/60 mmHg, P 102, moderate pale, mild jaundice, minimal ascites. Hct 24%, platelet 80,000, INR 1.7, PTT 43 sec. What blood component should be given first?
ai_notes: Cirrhotic patient with coagulopathy (elevated INR 1.7, PTT 43) and anemia. FFP contains multiple clotting factors and is indicated for coagulopathy in cirrhosis. While PRC may be needed eventually for anemia (Hct 24%), correcting coagulopathy first (FFP) is more urgent as it prevents further bleeding. The elevated INR and PTT are signs of hepatic synthetic dysfunction.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 34-year-old patient with underlying thalassemia underwent a blood transfusion. He developed back pain, fever, chill and dark urine 15 minutes after receiving transfusion. V/S BT 37°C, BP 110/70, PR 90, RR 20. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction: onset within 15 minutes of transfusion, dark urine (hemoglobinuria), back pain, fever, chills. This is a classic presentation of acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, usually due to ABO incompatibility or other major blood group incompatibility causing immediate RBC destruction.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A 70-year-old underwent hip surgery and had good mobilization post-operatively. After that, he developed DVT in the leg. For how long should anticoagulation be given?
ai_notes: Provoked DVT (surgery-related): According to standard guidelines, anticoagulation for provoked DVT secondary to transient risk factors (surgery) is typically 3 months. Indefinite anticoagulation is reserved for unprovoked DVT or recurrent events.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A man with underlying heart disease, status post CABG 3 years ago, presents with ecchymosis and left hemiparesis. CT shows right parietal intracerebral hemorrhage. His medication is warfarin. INR 5. What is the immediate management?
ai_notes: Warfarin-induced serious bleeding (ICH) with elevated INR: Immediate management requires rapid reversal of warfarin effect. FFP contains Vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X). In urgent situations like ICH, FFP or PCC (Prothrombin Complex Concentrate) is given immediately while Vitamin K (slower onset, 12-24 hours) is given concurrently. FFP is most appropriate choice from given options.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A woman diagnosed with deep vein thrombosis 3 months ago. She takes coumadin 3 mg daily. She has an accidentally fracture femur and has to operate within 24 hours. INR 3.0. What is the management?
ai_notes: Elective surgery within 24 hours in patient on warfarin with INR 3.0: IV Vitamin K is appropriate for non-emergency reversal. Since there is 24 hours before surgery, vitamin K (onset 12-24 hours) is sufficient time. FFP would be for urgent/emergency situations with serious bleeding. Vitamin K allows gradual reversal suitable for this timeframe.
subject: hemato_med

scenario: A patient on warfarin will undergo emergency laparotomy. What is the antidote for warfarin?
ai_notes: Vitamin K (phytonadione) is the specific antidote for warfarin overdose. While FFP provides immediate factor replacement in emergency settings, Vitamin K is the actual antidote that reverses warfarin's mechanism. For emergency surgery, both FFP (immediate) and Vitamin K (sustained effect) are often given together.

ai_notes: Microcytic anemia (MCV=70) with elevated RDW and normal WBC/platelets suggests iron deficiency anemia. Ferritin is the first-line investigation. Corrected choice D from 'directed comb test' to 'Direct antiglobulin test (Coombs test)'

scenario: 25-year-old female presented with progressive dyspnea for 7 days, petechiae and gingival bleeding for 2 days. Physical examination reveals moderate pallor, mild icteric sclerae, petechiae on both legs. CBC: Hb 6.5 g/dL, Hct 19%, WBC 5,200/μL (N 67%, L 35%, M 6%, E 2%), Plt 40,000/μL. Blood smear: NCNC, anisocytosis 3+, polychromasia 1+, microspherocytes 1+. Reticulocyte count elevated. Direct Coombs test positive. What is the most appropriate management?
explanation: This patient has autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) with Evans syndrome (concurrent thrombocytopenia), evidenced by anemia, jaundice, positive Coombs test, microspherocytes, and thrombocytopenia with bleeding manifestations. First-line treatment for AIHA is high-dose corticosteroid (dexamethasone or prednisolone) which suppresses autoimmune hemolysis. IVIG is second-line, and plasmapheresis is not indicated in AIHA. Transfusions should be avoided unless absolutely necessary due to transfusion reactions.

scenario: A 25-year-old female presents with progressive dyspnea for 7 days and purpura with bleeding gums for 2 days. Physical examination reveals moderate pallor, mild icteric sclerae, and petechiae on both legs. CBC: Hb 6.5 g/dL, Hct 19%, WBC 5,200/μL (N 67%, L 35%, M 6%, E 2%), Plt 40,000/μL. Blood smear shows NCNC (normocytic normochromic), anisocytosis 3+, polychromasia 1+, microspherocytes 1+. Reticulocyte count is elevated. What is the most appropriate management?
explanation: Clinical presentation is consistent with ITP (Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura) with autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) overlap. The microspherocytes, elevated reticulocyte count, jaundice, and severe anemia with thrombocytopenia (plt 40,000) suggest immune-mediated destruction. IVIG is first-line therapy for acute ITP as it blocks Fc receptors on macrophages, reducing antibody-mediated platelet destruction and providing rapid response within 24-48 hours. While corticosteroids can be used, IVIG is more appropriate for rapid hemostasis in acute bleeding.

choices.text: Spleenic sequestration จากการเพิ่มพูนของกระดูกอาศรัย (hyperplasia)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bone marrow suppression จากยาต้านวัณโรค

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilutional effect จากการสะสมของเหลวในช่องท้อง (ascites)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased production จากการบุกรุกของวัณโรคในกระดูกสันหลัง

explanation: ผู้ป่วยมี splenomegaly ขนาดใหญ่ที่มาพร้อมกับ pancytopenia เบาบาง ซึ่งเป็นลักษณะเฉพาะของ splenic sequestration ม้าน่า splenomegaly ทำให้อัตราการไหลผ่าน (blood flow) ช้า ส่งผลให้มีการเพิ่มพูนของเม็ดเลือดในม้าน่า และเกิด trapping ของเม็ดเลือดทั้งหมดสามประเภท (RBC, WBC, และ platelets) ทำให้ลดลงใน peripheral blood แม้ว่า bone marrow ทำงานปกติ ตัวเลือก D อาจมีได้แต่ผู้ป่วยไม่ได้ค่า ascites ชัดเจนจากการตรวจร่างกาย

scenario: ผู้ป่วยชายอายุ 35 ปี มาด้วยอาการเหนื่อยง่าย ซีดจัง และปวดศีรษะเรื้อรัง เลือดทั่วไปแสดง macrocytic anemia ค่า MCV = 120 fL ค่า reticulocyte count ต่ำ การตรวจ bone marrow biopsy พบ megaloblastic changes และ hypercellular marrow ข้อใดต่อไปนี้เป็นสาเหตุที่เกี่ยวข้องกับ DNA synthesis ผิดปกติมากที่สุด

choices.label: A
choices.text: วิตามิน B12 deficiency ทำให้ methionine synthase ไม่ทำงาน และลดการสร้าง dTMP ในการสังเคราะห์ DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Folate deficiency ทำให้ลดปริมาณ reduced folate cofactors ที่จำเป็นสำหรับ one-carbon transfer reaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leukemia ทำให้เพิ่มอัตราการสังเคราะห์ DNA แบบไม่ควบคุม

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypothyroidism ทำให้ลดการดูดซึมวิตามิน B12 ในลำไส้เล็ก

choices.label: E

explanation: Folate deficiency เป็นสาเหตุหลักของ megaloblastic anemia ที่มีเซลล์ bone marrow ที่ขยายตัวมากขึ้น (megaloblastic changes) เนื่องจาก folate เป็น coenzyme ที่จำเป็นสำหรับ one-carbon transfer reaction ในการสังเคราะห์ purine และ pyrimidine ซึ่งเป็นส่วนประกอบของ DNA ขาดfolate ทำให้ DNA synthesis ช้าลง แต่ RNA synthesis ยังคงเกิดขึ้นปกติ ส่งผลให้เซลล์โต (cytoplasm) แต่นิวเคลียสพัฒนาช้า

scenario: 35-year-old female presented with progressive dyspnea for 4 months, denies fever, anorexia, diarrhea, or vomiting. Physical examination reveals pallor and petechiae. Laboratory findings: Hb 8.5 g/dL, WBC 1,500/μL (neutrophils 15%, lymphocytes 75%), platelets 30,000/μL. Bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellular marrow with fatty infiltration and no fibrosis. What is your diagnosis?
explanation: Aplastic anemia presents with pancytopenia (anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) and hypocellular bone marrow without fibrosis or malignant infiltration. The clinical presentation of chronic dyspnea, absence of systemic symptoms, and laboratory findings of Hb 8.5, WBC 1,500 with relative lymphocytosis, and platelets 30,000 are classic for aplastic anemia. Bone marrow biopsy showing hypocellular marrow with fatty replacement (without blast excess or dysplasia) confirms the diagnosis.
